attributes.stem: An otherwise well 67-year-old store manager presents with increasing stiffness in his left hand (Image). He has limited ability to fully extend all his fingers and this has been impacting his work. About one year ago he got a puncture wound in the palm of his hand and this required a course of antibiotics. He does not use any medications and has no significant medical history. He drinks about four cans of beer at weekends and has a 15 pack-year smoking history.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely underlying cause of the problem?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's presentation is consistent with Dupuytren's contracture.

Dupuytren's contracture is a fibroproliferative disease of the hand that involves the deposition of collagen, leading to contractures which impact hand mobility and strength. The reported prevalence ranges from 2% to 42%.

The precise cause of Dupuytren's contracture is uncertain. Whilst many factors (smoking, diabetes mellitus, alcoholism, trauma) may contribute to the development of the condition, in most instances, there is a genetic predisposition. The condition tends to occur most often in men over the age of 50 of Northern European descent and there will often be more than one family member affected. Some studies have revealed a positive association with the disease and specific human leukocyte antigen systems (HLA-B12, A1, DR4, DR3, DR2 and DR1.

In this case, the appearance and colour of the patient's skin would suggest he is of Celtic origin. His alcohol consumption and/or smoking may have been a secondary triggering event, they are not unduly excessive. His genetic background is most likely to be the major factor in the pathogenesis of the Dupuytren disease.

The condition most commonly affects the ring and little fingers but may involve any digit. The disease usually begins at the palm and presents with distal progression.

The main goal of treatment is to improve digit extension and function. Early disease may be treated conservatively with physical therapy, as well as vitamin E or steroids - although evidence is inconclusive. More severe contractures may be treated medically with collagenase or surgically with fasciotomy.
learningPoints: A genetic predisposition may be a factor in many cases of Dupuytren's contracture.
references: Grazina R, Teixeira S, Ramos R, Sousa H, Ferreira A, Lemos R. Dupuytren's disease: where do we stand? EFORT Open Rev. 2019;4(2):63-9.
Rodrigues JN, Becker GW, Ball C, Zhang W, Giele H, Hobby J, et al. Surgery for Dupuytren's contracture of the fingers. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2015(12):CD010143.
